---
title: "Equation 3.17 — Residuals sum to zero with an intercept"
lean_name: "HansenEconometrics.residual_sum_zero_of_one_mem_colspan"
chapter: 3
---

## Textbook statement

For a design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times k}$ and response vector
$y \in \mathbb{R}^n$, when
[$X^\top X$ is invertible](../../assumptions/chapter3.qmd) and the constant
vector $\mathbf{1}$ lies in the column span of $X$, OLS residuals sum to zero:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n \hat e_i = 0.
$$

The "intercept" condition is formalized as a vector of coefficients $c$ with
$Xc=\mathbf{1}$.

### Proof sketch

The sum of residuals is the residual vector dotted with the all-ones vector:
$$
\sum_i \hat e_i = \hat e^\top \mathbf{1}.
$$
If $\mathbf{1}=Xc$, this becomes
$$
\hat e^\top Xc = (X^\top\hat e)^\top c.
$$
The normal equations say $X^\top\hat e=0$, so the expression is zero.

## Lean statement

The theorem lives in `HansenEconometrics/Chapter3LeastSquaresAlgebra.lean`:

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Hansen equation 3.17: residuals sum to zero when the design contains a constant.">theorem residual_sum_zero_of_one_mem_colspan</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The fixed design matrix and response vector.">(X : Matrix n k ℝ) (y : n → ℝ)</span> <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="Full-rank condition, represented by invertibility of X'X.">[Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]</span>
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The intercept condition: some coefficient vector c maps to the all-ones vector under X.">{c : k → ℝ} (hc : X *ᵥ c = 1)</span> :
    <span class="lean-token" tabindex="0" data-explain="The formal residual-sum statement. Finset summation ranges over the finite row index type n.">∑ i, residual X y i = 0</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

### Reading the statement

| Lean | Math | What it gives us |
|---|---|---|
| `{c : k → ℝ}` | coefficient vector $c$ | A witness that the constant vector is a linear combination of the columns of $X$. |
| `hc : X *ᵥ c = 1` | $Xc=\mathbf{1}$ | The intercept / constant-in-column-space assumption. See the [Chapter 3 assumption map](../../assumptions/chapter3.qmd). |
| `∑ i, residual X y i = 0` | $\sum_i \hat e_i=0$ | Residuals have mean zero up to the conventional factor $1/n$. |

## Translation notes

- Lean writes the all-ones vector simply as `1`, because vectors `n → ℝ` have a
  pointwise one.
- The theorem uses a concrete witness `c` rather than an existential
  hypothesis. That makes later proofs directly rewrite `1` as `X *ᵥ c`.
- This is a consequence of the normal equations, not an extra property of the
  objective function.

## Tactic-by-tactic walkthrough

Hover over a proof line to read what it contributes.

::: {.lean-hover-panel}
```{=html}
<pre><code><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="The theorem statement: if the constant vector is in the column span of X, OLS residuals sum to zero.">theorem residual_sum_zero_of_one_mem_colspan
    (X : Matrix n k ℝ) (y : n → ℝ) [Invertible (Xᵀ * X)]
    {c : k → ℝ} (hc : X *ᵥ c = 1) :
    ∑ i, residual X y i = 0 := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Start a calculation chain from the residual sum.">  calc</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Rewrite the sum of coordinates as the dot product with the all-ones vector.">    ∑ i, residual X y i = residual X y ⬝ᵥ 1 := by
      rw [dotProduct_one]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Use the intercept witness to replace the all-ones vector by X c.">    _ = residual X y ⬝ᵥ (X *ᵥ c) := by
      rw [hc]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Move X across the dot product: e'(Xc) becomes (e'X)c.">    _ = (Matrix.vecMul (residual X y) X) ⬝ᵥ c := by
      rw [Matrix.dotProduct_mulVec]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Finish by showing e'X is zero from the normal equations X'e = 0.">    _ = 0 := by</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Convert the normal equations from transpose-times-vector form into row-vector-times-matrix form.">      have h : Matrix.vecMul (residual X y) X = 0 := by
        simpa [vecMul_eq_mulVec_transpose] using (normal_equations X y)</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Replace e'X by zero.">      rw [h]</span><span class="lean-line" tabindex="0" data-explain="Simplify the zero dot product.">      simp</span>
</code></pre>
```
:::

## Downstream uses

This is the theorem behind the familiar statement that residuals average to
zero in a regression with an intercept. It also records the precise condition
needed by later finite-sample decomposition arguments: the all-ones vector must
be in the column space of the design.
